basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Balad — “The City” — opens with an oath upon Mecca and upon the Prophet who dwells there, and closes upon the striking image of a steep path to be climbed. Between the two, the sūra takes man in his rawest reality: laqad khalaqnā l-insāna fī kabad, “We have created man in toil” — in the midst of effort, of difficulty, almost of labour. Human life is, by nature, hard going. And God gives man what he needs to stand there: two eyes, a tongue, two lips, and the knowing of the two highways. Then He poses the decisive question: what have you made of all this? You have not stormed the steep path — fa-lā qtaḥama l-ʿaqaba. That steep path is then named: to free a slave, to feed the orphan, the poor, in the hard days; then to be of those believers who recommend to one another patience and mercy. But Qushayrī draws the verse toward its inmost sense, and his word is unforgettable: the steep path that man must storm is his soul and his passion (nafsuhu wa-hawāhu, نَفْسُهُ وَهَوَاهُ). Until he has crossed that pass, he does not prosper. And he adds: to free a slave is to free one's soul from the bondage of aims and persons. It is also to leave the darkness of choice (ikhtiyār) for the breadth of contentment (riḍā). The whole spiritual life holds in this image of a pass to be crossed.

versets.trad: No! I swear by this city!
versets.commentaire: lā uqsimu (لَا أُقْسِمُ): an oath preceded by an apparently negative lā. Arabic grammar allows it as emphasis: not “I do not swear,” but “I do indeed swear.” The city — al-balad — is Mecca, the sanctuary. Qushayrī: “He swears by this city, that is, Mecca.”

mots.def: lā: here an emphatic particle reinforcing the oath that follows.
aqsama: to swear; imperfect 1st pers. sing.
balad: city, land, country; here Mecca.

versets.trad: — and you, you are a resident of this city,
versets.commentaire: ḥill (حِلّ) carries both senses of “one who has come down, who dwells” and “one for whom things are made lawful.” Qushayrī gives both nuances: “It was made lawful for him only one hour” — an allusion to the conquest of Mecca, when the Prophet was permitted to fight within its sanctuary. Then he adds the splendid word: “He swears by this city because you dwell in it — and the city of the Beloved is itself beloved (balad al-ḥabīb ḥabīb, بَلَد الحَبِيب حَبِيب).” The holiness of a place comes from Him whom it shelters. What lies near a saint becomes itself saintly.

mots.def: ḥill: one who dwells, who has come down to a place; also: one for whom a thing is lawful.

versets.trad: by the begetter and what he begets!
versets.commentaire: wālid (وَالِد): begetter, father; mā walada, “what he has begotten.” Qushayrī opens two readings: every father and every child — the expression then covers the whole human line; or Adam and his offspring — that is, the whole of humanity taken as one family. Either way, the oath holds together the city and the line: the place and the time of men. And the verdict is about to fall.

mots.def: wālid: active participle of walada (to beget); the father.

versets.trad: Truly We have created man in toil.
versets.commentaire: kabad (كَبَد) — a word that says it all. The root names the liver, but also effort, trial, the burden that weighs upon the central organ. The word covers birth in pain, growth in difficulty, life that is never without tension. The sūra does not play easy consolation: it names from the outset that the human condition is in hardship, and not merely traversed by it. If the sūra will then speak of a path to climb, it is because it first acknowledges that everything, in human life, is already an ascent.

mots.def: kabad: effort, trial; root that names the liver as the seat of inner strain.

versets.ar: أَيَحْسَبُ أَن لَّن يَقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ أَحَدٌ
versets.tr: a-yaḥsabu an lan yaqdira ʿalayhi aḥad
versets.trad: Does he think no one has power over him?
versets.commentaire: A rhetorical question addressed to the man who thinks himself beyond reach. The verbal form is fine: a-yaḥsabu, “does he think within himself?”. The sūra does not target a noisy discourse — it ferrets out a secret belief. Deep within, man often imagines that nothing can seize him. And this silent belief is the spring of all his pride.

mots.def: ḥasiba: to think, to presume; imperfect.
qadara: to have power, capacity; subjunctive.
aḥad: anyone (in interrogative/negative context: no one).

versets.trad: He says: “I have squandered abundant wealth!”
versets.commentaire: lubad (لُبَد): heaped up, piled in thick layers. Tradition reports that this verse came down concerning a certain man of the Banū Jumaḥ, nicknamed Abū al-Ashaddayn, who used to boast of having spent vast sums against the Prophet. The verse arrests the boasting itself: not the act, but the word that glorifies it. Spending is not evil in itself; what is evil is to take it as a title to glory.

mots.def: ahlaka: to make perish, to squander; perfect 1st pers. sing.
lubad: heaped up, piled in thick layers.

versets.trad: Does he imagine that no one has seen him?
versets.commentaire: The verse completes the previous one. To the one who struts outwardly answers the one who believes, inwardly, that no one has seen what he has done. Here too the sūra unveils an intimate illusion. At his deepest, man imagines that his acts remain his alone, and that no eye has access to them. The following verse will open the reply: did We not give you eyes?

mots.def: raʾā: to see; apocopated imperfect + suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: apocopated imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: Have We not given him two eyes,
versets.commentaire: The verse pivots by listing the gifts received. ʿaynayn (عَيْنَيْنِ): two eyes — in the dual, a grammatical form proper to Arabic that underlines the pair. The man who thought he had been seen by no one receives the reply: you yourself have been given two eyes with which to see. Every faculty entrusted to you is also a mode by which God holds you before Him.

mots.def: jaʿala: to make, to set, to give; apocopated imperfect 1st pers. plur.

versets.trad: a tongue and two lips
versets.commentaire: lisān (لِسَان), the tongue; shafatayn (شَفَتَيْنِ), the two lips. As with the eyes, the precision is meticulous. The tongue is one, but the lips are two — to make the fold, to contain, to hold back. Human speech is thus double in its envelope: the voice that comes out and the lips that can close. It is in this very structure that silence becomes possible. The wisdom of speech rests not only on what one says, but on what one can keep silent.

versets.trad: and have We not guided him to the meeting of the two highways?
versets.commentaire: al-najdayn (النَّجْدَيْنِ): the two heights, the two clearly visible paths; in the dual once again. Najd names elevated ground from which one sees clearly, or the path that stands out. Commentators are unanimous: these are the two ways — that of good and that of evil — to which man has been guided beforehand. It is the exact echo of verse 8 of sūra 91: He inspired in it its transgression and its godfearing. Man is not, then, in the darkness of the unknown. He knows. His whole drama is to choose.

mots.def: hadā: to guide; perfect 1st pers. plur. + suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 1st pers. plur.

mots.sens: the two paths, the two heights
mots.def: najd: height, elevated ground from which the view is clear, the clear path; in the definite dual.

versets.trad: But he has not stormed the steep path!
versets.commentaire: A major verse. iqtaḥama (اقْتَحَمَ): to storm, to assault, to charge into (a difficult place). al-ʿaqaba (العَقَبَة): the pass, the steep way, the difficult ascent. The verse reproaches man for not having faced this climb. Qushayrī here performs the decisive turn: “The steep path that man is bound to storm is his own soul and his passion (nafsuhu wa-hawāhu, نَفْسُهُ وَهَوَاهُ). And until he has crossed that pass, he does not prosper.” The ʿaqaba is not outside: it is within. The true ascent is not that of acts — it is the one that consists in rising above one's own lower soul. The whole of Sufi teaching gathers in this verse.

mots.def: iqtaḥama: intensive form; to charge into, to assault, to storm a difficult place.
ʿaqaba: pass, steep way, difficult climb between two mountains.

versets.trad: And what will let you know what the steep path is?
versets.commentaire: A recurring formula of the Quran — mā adrāka — to amplify what follows. The ʿaqaba is not a way one already knows; it must be named. The sūra is about to do so in five acts, each a rung of the same pass.

mots.def: adrā: to make known; perfect + suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: It is to set a captive free,
versets.commentaire: fakk raqaba (فَكُّ رَقَبَة): “the freeing of a neck” — the Arabic phrase for the manumission of a slave. But Qushayrī here too unfolds the sense inward: “The freeing of the neck is to free one's soul from the bondage of aims and persons (riqq al-aghrāḍ wa-l-ashkhāṣ, رِقّ الأَغْرَاض وَالأَشْخَاص).” Bondage of aims — running after what one has set as one's goal. Bondage of persons — depending on the gaze and the judgment of others. From these two slaveries, above all, must one first be freed. And he adds, finer still: “It is also to leave the darkness of choice (ikhtiyār, اخْتِيَار) for the breadth of contentment (riḍā, رِضَا)” — to step out of that restlessness where every move is a calculation, and to enter the calm in which one receives what comes.

mots.def: fakk: verbal noun of fakka; to untie, to free.
raqaba: the neck; by metonymy, the slave (freed by being untied).

versets.ar: أَوْ إِطْعَامٌ فِي يَوْمٍ ذِي مَسْغَبَةٍ
versets.tr: aw iṭʿāmun fī yawmin dhī masghaba
versets.trad: or to feed, on a day of famine,
versets.commentaire: masghaba (مَسْغَبَة): hunger, famine. Qushayrī adds an admirable touch: “He feeds the one in famine while he himself is in hunger.” To give when one is oneself in need — to give precisely because the other is hungry and one knows what that is. The act takes its full weight in this context of shared lack.

mots.def: iṭʿām: verbal noun of aṭʿama; to feed, to give to eat.

versets.trad: an orphan close of kin,
versets.commentaire: yatīm dhū maqraba: an orphan endowed with closeness — that is, an orphan who is also of one's family. The choice in the verse is subtle: the precision “close kinsman” multiplies the merit, for alms to a member of one's own family combine, in Islam, charity and the maintenance of blood ties (ṣilat al-raḥim). The other side: these are also those whom family closeness sometimes makes harder to help than strangers, through shame or old resentment.

versets.trad: or a poor man in the dust.
versets.commentaire: miskīn dhū matraba: a poor man endowed with dust — a splendid and terrible image. Qushayrī: “He has nothing, to the point that one might say he has stuck to the earth from hunger.” The poor man takes on the colour of the ground. The verse does not describe an abstract poverty: it describes that particular body, brought so low that it almost becomes one with the dust. To feed that — is what the steep path asks.

mots.def: miskīn: poor, destitute; literally “motionless” from misery.
matraba: the state of being so poor as to seem stuck to the dust (turāb).

versets.ar: ثُمَّ كَانَ مِنَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالْمَرْحَمَةِ
versets.tr: thumma kāna mina lladhīna āmanū wa-tawāṣaw bi-ṣ-ṣabri wa-tawāṣaw bi-l-marḥama
versets.trad: Then to be among those who believe and exhort one another to patience, and exhort one another to mercy.
versets.commentaire: The verse completes the steep path: isolated acts (freeing, feeding) do not suffice — they must be set into a life of faith held by patience and reciprocal mercy. tawāṣaw (تَوَاصَوْا): they commended one another. The same verb as in sūra 103 (al-ʿAṣr), where it describes spiritual companionship. Qushayrī adds an essential note: “thumma kāna — then he was — means: his khātima (خَاتِمَة, the closing of his life) be upon that.” The image is not only of doing one charitable act; it is of ending one's life upon this path. The steep path is climbed over time.

mots.def: tawāṣā: reciprocal form of waṣṣā; to commend one another, to pass on as testament.
mots.gram: reciprocal perfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. plur.
reciprocal perfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: marḥama: active mercy toward creatures; same root as raḥma.

versets.trad: These are the companions of the right.
versets.commentaire: aṣḥāb al-maymana (أَصْحَاب المَيْمَنَة): the companions of the right — on the Day of Resurrection, those who receive their book in the right hand. But the word maymana covers wider: yumn is also felicity, blessing. Qushayrī: “these are the people of happiness and blessing.” In the Quran, to go to the right is to go toward life.

mots.def: aṣḥāb: plural of ṣāḥib; the companions.
maymana: the right hand; also felicity, blessing. Root y-m-n.

versets.ar: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآيَاتِنَا هُمْ أَصْحَابُ الْمَشْأَمَةِ
versets.tr: wa-lladhīna kafarū bi-āyātinā hum aṣḥābu l-mashʾama
versets.trad: But those who disbelieve in Our signs, they are the companions of the left:
versets.commentaire: al-mashʾama (المَشْأَمَة): the left, but also misfortune (shuʾm). Qushayrī: “they are unfortunate for their own souls.” As for the right, the word covers both senses: the geographical place on the Day, and the spiritual state. And always the same logic: the lot man receives is not a fate imposed from elsewhere — it is, in the end, the rendered image of what he made of himself.

mots.def: āyāt: signs, verses; with plural suffix pronoun.
mashʾama: the left hand; also misfortune. Root sh-ʾ-m (ill omen).

versets.trad: a Fire shall close in upon them!
versets.commentaire: muʾṣada (مُؤْصَدَة): closed, sealed, with shut doors. Qushayrī: “A fire brought down upon them — that is, whose doors are closed.” The image is not only that of the Fire, but of the closing. Misfortune, in the Quran, is rarely described by its heat; it is described by its lack of an exit. By contrast, the whole sūra has been the effort to climb a path that itself opens upward. Qushayrī closes, as with a seal: “And the steep path that man must storm is his soul and his passion.”

mots.def: muʾṣad: closed, sealed, with shut doors; passive participle of awṣada.
